items.question: Promise.resolve().then(A); setTimeout(B, 0); — which callback runs first after the current script?
options: A — microtasks drain completely before the next task
items.explanation: The microtask queue (promises) empties after the current task; setTimeout callbacks wait in the task queue behind it.
items.relatedLessonTitle: The JavaScript Event Loop: Tasks and Microtasks

items.question: for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) setTimeout(() => console.log(i)); prints 3,3,3 because:
options: All three closures capture the same variable binding, read after the loop finished — let creates a fresh binding per iteration
items.explanation: Closures capture variables, not values. let's per-iteration scoping prints 0,1,2.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Closures: The Feature Behind Everything

items.question: A <div (click)="save()"> instead of <button> is an accessibility bug because:
options: It's unfocusable, ignores Enter/Space, and exposes no role — keyboard and screen-reader users can't use it
items.explanation: Native button brings focus, keyboard activation, and semantics for free.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Accessibility: The Engineering Basics

items.question: In flexbox, justify-content and align-items control:
options: Main-axis distribution and cross-axis alignment respectively
items.explanation: And flex-direction decides which axis is which — always ask 'which axis is main right now?'
items.relatedLessonTitle: CSS Flexbox: One-Dimensional Layout Mastery

items.question: rem units are preferred for font sizes because:
options: They respect the user's browser font-size setting — an accessibility win
items.explanation: rem scales with user preferences; px ignores them. Use px only for borders and fine details.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Responsive Design: Container Queries and Fluid Sizing

items.question: this.http.post('/api/orders', order); with no .subscribe() results in:
options: The request fires, response is dropped
No request at all — observables are lazy; subscription starts execution
items.explanation: Cold observables are recipes; subscribe is 'cook'. The silently-never-sent save is a rite of passage.
items.relatedLessonTitle: RxJS Foundations: Observables, Subscriptions, Laziness

items.question: track item.id in @for matters because without meaningful tracking:
options: Replacing the array immutably destroys and recreates every DOM row instead of reusing unchanged ones
items.explanation: Immutable updates produce new references by design; track restores row identity across replacements.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Rendering Lists: @for, trackBy, and Virtual Scrolling

items.question: An OnPush component's list doesn't update after this.items.push(newItem) because:
options: OnPush compares input references — mutation keeps the same array reference, so the check is skipped
items.explanation: OnPush's contract is immutability: replace the reference (spread/concat) so === sees the change.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Angular Change Detection: Zone.js and OnPush

items.question: Search-as-you-type uses switchMap because:
options: Each new query cancels the in-flight request — no stale response can overwrite results for a newer query
items.explanation: Out-of-order responses are the killer bug of naive typeahead; switch semantics make 'latest wins' structural.
items.relatedLessonTitle: RxJS Flattening: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap

items.question: The most leak-proof way to render stream data in a template is:
options: subscribe in ngOnInit and assign a field
toSignal or the async pipe — the framework manages the subscription with the view lifecycle
items.explanation: Declarative binding removes the manual lifecycle entirely — no subscription you write, none you forget to dispose.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Subscription Leaks and Lifecycle: takeUntilDestroyed

items.question: computed() signal dependencies are determined by:
options: Whatever signals the function actually reads during execution — tracked automatically
items.explanation: Reading a signal inside a reactive context registers the dependency — even conditionally.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Angular Signals: Fine-Grained Reactivity

items.question: Two async pipes bound to the same raw http.get observable cause:
options: Two actual HTTP requests — cold observables re-execute per subscriber; shareReplay fixes it
items.explanation: Each subscription runs the recipe again. shareReplay({bufferSize:1, refCount:true}) multicasts one execution.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Subjects and Multicasting: Sharing One Execution

items.question: canMatch beats canActivate for guarding lazy admin routes because:
options: It rejects before the chunk downloads — unauthorized users never even fetch the admin bundle
items.explanation: canMatch gates route matching itself (and thus loadChildren); canActivate runs after the code loaded.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Lazy Loading and Route-Level Code Splitting

items.question: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr)) gives responsive card grids without media queries because:
options: The browser fits as many ≥280px tracks as width allows and stretches them — continuous reflow
items.explanation: The constraint encodes the intent; the engine solves it at every width instead of at discrete breakpoints.
items.relatedLessonTitle: CSS Grid: Two-Dimensional Layout

items.question: A submit button wired with mergeMap (or switchMap) lets an impatient user cause duplicate orders. The correct operator is:
options: exhaustMap — ignore new clicks while the first save is in flight (plus server-side idempotency keys)
items.explanation: merge runs every click in parallel; switch 'cancels' client-side but the request may still reach the server. exhaust ignores extras.
items.relatedLessonTitle: RxJS Flattening: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap

items.question: Animating with transform/opacity stays smooth under load because:
options: They run in a Web Worker
They skip layout AND paint — the compositor moves existing GPU layers, often off the main thread
items.explanation: Composite-only changes are the cheapest pipeline tier — animating left/width reflows every frame instead.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Browser Rendering: Reflow, Repaint, Compositing

items.question: Storing JWTs in localStorage is risky because:
options: Any successful XSS reads it silently — HttpOnly cookies / the BFF pattern keep tokens out of JavaScript's reach
items.explanation: Script-readable storage turns one XSS into full token theft; HttpOnly cookies remove the bulk-exfiltration payoff.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Frontend Security: XSS, Sanitization, CSP

items.question: Copying server data (product lists, orders) into a global NgRx/signals store by default is problematic because:
options: You've re-created cache invalidation — staleness, refetching, consistency — without the tooling a fetch-cache layer provides
items.explanation: The server owns that truth; treat it as cached server state, and reserve the store for true client state (session, cart, preferences).
items.relatedLessonTitle: State Management: Signals Stores vs NgRx

items.question: Reading offsetHeight inside a loop that also writes styles causes:
options: Forced synchronous layout per iteration (layout thrashing) — batch all reads before all writes
items.explanation: Geometry reads on a dirty tree force immediate layout; interleaving multiplies it. DevTools flags 'Forced reflow'.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Browser Rendering: Reflow, Repaint, Compositing

items.question: Angular's TransferState exists because without it:
options: Every HTTP call made during server rendering repeats on client boot — double API load and a visible flicker
items.explanation: Server-fetched responses are serialized into the HTML so the client's HttpClient answers from the embedded cache.
items.relatedLessonTitle: SSR and Hydration: When the Server Renders Angular
